* A custom error type for failed pipeline requests.
* The code of the error itself (use statics on RestError if possible.)
* Bonus property set by the throw site.
* This means that parsing the response from the server failed. It may have been malformed.
* Something went wrong when making the request. This means the actual request failed for some reason, such as a DNS issue or the connection being lost.

* Determines if this policy be applied before or after retry logic. Only use `perRetry` if you need to modify the request again each time the operation is retried due to retryable service issues.

* An interface compatible with NodeJS's `http.Agent`. We want to avoid publicly re-exporting the actual interface, since it might vary across runtime versions.
* For agents with keepAlive enabled, this sets the maximum number of sockets that will be left open in the free state.
* Determines how many concurrent sockets the agent can have open per origin.
* An object which contains queues of requests that have not yet been assigned to sockets.
* An object which contains arrays of sockets currently in use by the agent.
* Destroy any sockets that are currently in use by the agent.
* Represents API Key authentication scheme. API Key authentication requires a key to be provided with each request. The key can be provided in different locations: query parameter, header, or cookie.
* Name of the API key parameter
* Credential for API Key authentication. Provides an API key that will be used in the request headers.
* Represents OAuth2 Authorization Code flow configuration.

* Represents HTTP Basic authentication scheme. Basic authentication scheme requires a username and password to be provided with each request. The credentials are encoded using Base64 and included in the Authorization header.
* Credential for HTTP Basic authentication. Provides username and password for basic authentication headers.

* Represents HTTP Bearer authentication scheme. Bearer authentication scheme requires a bearer token to be provided with each request. The token is included in the Authorization header with the "Bearer" prefix.
* Gets a Bearer token for the specified flows.
* A part of the request body in a multipart request.
* The body of this part of the multipart request.
* The headers for this part of the multipart request.
* Shape of a Rest Level Client
* This method will be used to send request that would check the path to provide strong types. When used by the codegen this type gets overridden with the generated types. For example:
* This method allows arbitrary paths and doesn't provide strong types
* The pipeline used by this client to make requests
* Represents OAuth2 Client Credentials flow configuration.
* A log function that can be dynamically enabled and redirected.
* True if this logger is active and logging.
* Extends this logger with a child namespace. Namespaces are separated with a ':' character.
* The current log method. Can be overridden to redirect output.
* Logs the given arguments to the `log` method.
* Wrapper object for http request and response. Deserialized object is stored in the `parsedBody` property when the response body is received in JSON.
* The response body as parsed JSON.
* The request that generated the response.
* Options used when creating and sending get bearer token requests for this operation.
* Options used when creating and sending get OAuth 2 requests for this operation.
* The required interface for a client that makes HTTP requests on behalf of a pipeline.
* The method that makes the request and returns a response.

* Removes a specific header from the collection.
* @param name - The name of the header to delete.
* Returns the value of a specific header or undefined if not set.
* @param name - The name of the header to retrieve.
* Returns true if the specified header exists.
* @param name - The name of the header to check.
* Sets a specific header with a given value.
* @param name - The name of the header to set.
* @param value - The value to use for the header.
* Accesses a raw JS object that acts as a simple map of header names to values.
* An interface compatible with NodeJS's `tls.KeyObject`. We want to avoid publicly re-exporting the actual interface, since it might vary across runtime versions.

* Header names whose values will be logged when logging is enabled. Defaults include a list of well-known safe headers. Any headers specified in this field will be added to that list. Any other values will be written to logs as "REDACTED".
* Query string names whose values will be logged when logging is enabled. By default no query string values are logged.
* The log function to use for writing pipeline logs. Defaults to core-http's built-in logger. Compatible with the `debug` library.
* A request body consisting of multiple parts.
* The boundary separating each part of the request body. If not specified, a random boundary will be generated.
* When specified, '--' will be prepended to the boundary in the request to ensure the boundary follows the specification.
* The parts of the request body.

* @param flows - The OAuth2 flows to use.

* The signal which can be used to abort requests.
* A function to be called each time a response is received from the server while performing the requested operation. May be called multiple times.
* Options used when creating and sending HTTP requests for this operation.
* Options used when creating and sending HTTP requests for this operation.
* Set to true if the request is sent over HTTP instead of HTTPS
* User defined custom request headers that will be applied before the request is sent.
* Callback which fires upon download progress.
* Callback which fires upon upload progress.
* Set to true if you want to skip encoding the path parameters
* The number of milliseconds a request can take before automatically being terminated.
* An object that can be passed as a path parameter, allowing for additional options to be set relating to how the parameter is encoded.
* Whether to allow for reserved characters in the value. If set to true, special characters such as '/' in the parameter's value will not be URL encoded. Defaults to false.
* Represents a pipeline for making a HTTP request to a URL. Pipelines can have multiple policies to manage manipulating each request before and after it is made to the server.
* Add a new policy to the pipeline.
* @param policy - A policy that manipulates a request.
* @param options - A set of options for when the policy should run.
* Duplicates this pipeline to allow for modifying an existing one without mutating it.
* Returns the current set of policies in the pipeline in the order in which they will be applied to the request. Later in the list is closer to when the request is performed.
* Remove a policy from the pipeline.
* @param options - Options that let you specify which policies to remove.
* Uses the pipeline to make a HTTP request.
* @param httpClient - The HttpClient that actually performs the request.
* @param request - The request to be made.
* Defines options that are used to configure the HTTP pipeline for an SDK client.
* Options for configuring Agent instance for outgoing requests
* Options to configure a proxy for outgoing requests.
* Options for how redirect responses are handled.
* Options that control how to retry failed requests.
* Options for setting common telemetry and tracing info to outgoing requests.
* Options for adding user agent details to outgoing requests.

* A pipeline policy manipulates a request as it travels through the pipeline. It is conceptually a middleware that is allowed to modify the request before it is made as well as the response when it is received.
* The policy name. Must be a unique string in the pipeline.
* The main method to implement that manipulates a request/response.
* @param next - The next policy in the pipeline. Must be called to continue the pipeline.
* Metadata about a request being made by the pipeline.
* Used to abort the request later.
* A Node-only option to provide a custom `http.Agent`/`https.Agent`. Does nothing when running in the browser.
* Set to true if the request is sent over HTTP instead of HTTPS
* List of authentication schemes used for this specific request. These schemes define how the request will be authenticated.

* If values are provided, these schemes override the client level authentication schemes. If an empty array is provided, it explicitly specifies no authentication for the request. If not provided at the request level, the client level authentication schemes will be used.
* If the connection should not be reused.
* A browser only option to enable browser Streams. If this option is set and a response is a stream the response will have a property `browserStream` instead of `blobBody` which will be undefined.
* To simulate a browser form post
* The HTTP headers to use when making the request.
* The HTTP method to use when making the request.
* Callback which fires upon download progress.
* Callback which fires upon upload progress.
* A unique identifier for the request. Used for logging and tracing.
* Additional options to set on the request. This provides a way to override existing ones or provide request properties that are not declared.

* For possible valid properties, see - NodeJS https.request options: https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#httprequestoptions-callback - Browser RequestInit: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/RequestInit
* WARNING: Options specified here will override any properties of same names when request is sent by {@link HttpClient}.

* A list of response status codes whose corresponding PipelineResponse body should be treated as a stream. When streamResponseStatusCodes contains the value Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY any status would be treated as a stream.
* The number of milliseconds a request can take before automatically being terminated. If the request is terminated, an `AbortError` is thrown. Defaults to 0, which disables the timeout.
* The URL to make the request to.
* Indicates whether the user agent should send cookies from the other domain in the case of cross-origin requests. Defaults to false.
* Settings to initialize a request. Almost equivalent to Partial<PipelineRequest>, but url is mandatory.
* Used to abort the request later.
* Set to true if the request is sent over HTTP instead of HTTPS
* List of authentication schemes used for this specific request. These schemes define how the request will be authenticated.

* If values are provided, these schemes override the client level authentication schemes. If an empty array is provided, it explicitly specifies no authentication for the request. If not provided at the request level, the client level authentication schemes will be used.
* If the connection should not be reused.

* A browser only option to enable use of the Streams API. If this option is set and streaming is used (see `streamResponseStatusCodes`), the response will have a property `browserStream` instead of `blobBody` which will be undefined.
* To simulate a browser form post
* The HTTP headers to use when making the request.
* The HTTP method to use when making the request.
* Callback which fires upon download progress.
* Callback which fires upon upload progress.
* A unique identifier for the request. Used for logging and tracing.
* Additional options to set on the request. This provides a way to override existing ones or provide request properties that are not declared.

* For possible valid properties, see - NodeJS https.request options: https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#httprequestoptions-callback - Browser RequestInit: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/RequestInit
* WARNING: Options specified here will override any properties of same names when request is sent by {@link HttpClient}.
* A list of response status codes whose corresponding PipelineResponse body should be treated as a stream.
* The number of milliseconds a request can take before automatically being terminated. If the request is terminated, an `AbortError` is thrown. Defaults to 0, which disables the timeout.
* The URL to make the request to.
* If credentials (cookies) should be sent along during an XHR. Defaults to false.
* Metadata about a response received by the pipeline.
* The response body as a browser Blob. Always undefined in node.js.
* The response body as text (string format)
* The response body as a browser ReadableStream. Always undefined in node.js.
* The response body as a node.js Readable stream. Always undefined in the browser.
* The request that generated this response.
* The HTTP status code of the response.
* Options that control how to retry failed requests.
* The maximum number of retry attempts. Defaults to 3.
* The maximum delay in milliseconds allowed before retrying an operation. Defaults to 64000 (64 seconds).
* The amount of delay in milliseconds between retry attempts. Defaults to 1000 (1 second). The delay increases exponentially with each retry up to a maximum specified by maxRetryDelayInMs.
* Options to configure a proxy for outgoing requests (Node.js only).
* The proxy's host address. Must include the protocol (e.g., http:// or https://).
* The password to authenticate with the proxy, if required.
* The user name to authenticate with the proxy, if required.
* An interface compatible with NodeJS's `tls.PxfObject`. We want to avoid publicly re-exporting the actual interface, since it might vary across runtime versions.
* PFX or PKCS12 encoded private key and certificate chain.
* Options for how redirect responses are handled.

* Whether to follow redirects to a different origin (scheme + host + port). When false (the default), cross-origin redirects are not followed and the redirect response is returned directly to the caller. Defaults to false.
* The maximum number of times the redirect URL will be tried before failing. Defaults to 20.
* Defines the methods that can be called on a resource
* Definition of the DELETE HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the GET HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the HEAD HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the OPTIONS HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the PATCH HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the POST HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the PUT HTTP method for a resource
* Definition of the TRACE HTTP method for a resource

* The HTTP status code of the request (if applicable.)
* Defines options that are used to configure common telemetry and tracing info
* The name of the header to pass the request ID to.
* Represents a certificate for TLS authentication.

* Optionally override the trusted CA certificates. Default is to trust the well-known CAs curated by Mozilla. Mozilla's CAs are completely replaced when CAs are explicitly specified using this option.

* Cert chains in PEM format. One cert chain should be provided per private key. Each cert chain should consist of the PEM formatted certificate for a provided private key, followed by the PEM formatted intermediate certificates (if any), in order, and not including the root CA (the root CA must be pre-known to the peer, see ca). When providing multiple cert chains, they do not have to be in the same order as their private keys in key. If the intermediate certificates are not provided, the peer will not be able to validate the certificate, and the handshake will fail.

* Private keys in PEM format. PEM allows the option of private keys being encrypted. Encrypted keys will be decrypted with options.passphrase. Multiple keys using different algorithms can be provided either as an array of unencrypted key strings or buffers, or an array of objects in the form `{pem: <string|buffer>[,passphrase: <string>]}`. The object form can only occur in an array.object.passphrase is optional. Encrypted keys will be decrypted with object.passphrase if provided, or options.passphrase if it is not.
* Shared passphrase used for a single private key and/or a PFX.

* PFX or PKCS12 encoded private key and certificate chain. pfx is an alternative to providing key and cert individually. PFX is usually encrypted, if it is, passphrase will be used to decrypt it. Multiple PFX can be provided either as an array of unencrypted PFX buffers, or an array of objects in the form `{buf: <string|buffer>[,passphrase: <string>]}`. The object form can only occur in an array.object.passphrase is optional. Encrypted PFX will be decrypted with object.passphrase if provided, or options.passphrase if it is not.
* Defines the methods available on the SDK-facing logger.
* Used for failures the program is unlikely to recover from, such as Out of Memory.
* Used when a function operates normally.
* Used for detailed troubleshooting scenarios. This is intended for use by developers / system administrators for diagnosing specific failures.
* Used when a function fails to perform its intended task. Usually this means the function will throw an exception. Not used for self-healing events (e.g. automatic retry)
* Options for adding user agent details to outgoing requests.
* String prefix to add to the user agent for outgoing requests. Defaults to an empty string.
* Union type of all supported authentication schemes
* Union type of all supported authentication credentials.
* General options that a Rest Level Client can take
* A simple object that provides form data, as if from a browser form.
* Each form data entry can be a string, Blob, or a File. If you wish to pass a file with a name but do not have access to the File class, you can use the createFile helper to create one.
* Http Response which body is a NodeJS stream object
* Supported HTTP methods to use when making requests.
* Http Response which body is a NodeJS stream object
* Represents the shape of an HttpResponse
* Union type of all supported OAuth2 flows
* Helper type used to detect parameters in a path template text surrounded by \{\} will be considered a path parameter
* Type to use with pathUnchecked, overrides the body type to any to allow flexibility
* Policies are executed in phases. The execution order is: 1. Serialize Phase 2. Policies not in a phase 3. Deserialize Phase 4. Retry Phase 5. Sign Phase
* A HttpHeaders collection represented as a simple JSON object.
* A HttpHeaders collection for input, represented as a simple JSON object.
* A function to be called each time a response is received from the server while performing the requested operation. May be called multiple times.

* Types of bodies supported on the request. NodeJS.ReadableStream and () =\> NodeJS.ReadableStream is Node only. Blob, ReadableStream<Uint8Array>, and () =\> ReadableStream<Uint8Array> are browser only.
* Shape of the default request parameters, this may be overridden by the specific request types to provide strong types
* A simple interface for making a pipeline request and receiving a response.
* Defines the type for a method that supports getting the response body as a raw stream
* Fired in response to upload or download progress.
* A TypeSpecRuntimeClientLogger is a function that can log to an appropriate severity level.
* The log levels supported by the logger. The log levels in order of most verbose to least verbose are: - verbose - info - warning - error
* Immediately enables logging at the specified log level. If no level is specified, logging is disabled.
* @param level - The log level to enable for logging. Options from most verbose to least verbose are: - verbose - info - warning - error
* Creates a logger context base on the provided options.
* @param options - The options for creating a logger context.
* The name of the environment variable to check for the log level.
* The TypeSpecRuntimeClientLogger provides a mechanism for overriding where logs are output to. By default, logs are sent to stderr. Override the `log` method to redirect logs to another location.
* Creates a logger for use by the SDKs that inherits from `TypeSpecRuntimeLogger`.
* @param namespace - The name of the SDK package.
* Retrieves the currently specified log level.
* Immediately enables logging at the specified log level. If no level is specified, logging is disabled.
* @param level - The log level to enable for logging. Options from most verbose to least verbose are: - verbose - info - warning - error
* Gets a pipeline policy that sets http.agent
* A policy to enable response decompression according to Accept-Encoding header https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Accept-Encoding

* A policy that retries according to three strategies: - When the server sends a 429 response with a Retry-After header. - When there are errors in the underlying transport layer (e.g. DNS lookup failures). - Or otherwise if the outgoing request fails, it will retry with an exponentially increasing delay.
* A policy that attempts to retry requests while introducing an exponentially increasing delay.
* @param options - Options that configure retry logic.
* A policy that encodes FormData on the request into the body.

* This method converts a proxy url into `ProxySettings` for use with ProxyPolicy. If no argument is given, it attempts to parse a proxy URL from the environment variables `HTTPS_PROXY` or `HTTP_PROXY`.
* - Internally this method is no longer necessary when setting proxy information.
* @param proxyUrl - The url of the proxy to use. May contain authentication information.
* A policy that logs all requests and responses.
* A policy that allows one to apply proxy settings to all requests. If not passed static settings, they will be retrieved from the HTTPS_PROXY or HTTP_PROXY environment variables.
* @param proxySettings - ProxySettings to use on each request.
* @param options - additional settings, for example, custom NO_PROXY patterns
* A policy to follow Location headers from the server in order to support server-side redirection. In the browser, this policy is not used.
* @param options - Options to control policy behavior.
* retryPolicy is a generic policy to enable retrying requests when certain conditions are met
* A retry policy that specifically seeks to handle errors in the underlying transport layer (e.g. DNS lookup failures) rather than retryable error codes from the server itself.
* @param options - Options that customize the policy.
* A policy that retries when the server sends a 429 response with a Retry-After header.

* To learn more, please refer to https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-resource-manager/resource-manager-request-limits, https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-subscription-service-limits and https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/virtual-machines/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-throttling-errors
* @param options - Options that configure retry logic.

* A policy that sets the User-Agent header (or equivalent) to reflect the library version.
* @param options - Options to customize the user agent value.
* Options that control how to retry failed requests.
* Options that control how to retry failed requests.
* The maximum number of retry attempts. Defaults to 3.
* The maximum delay in milliseconds allowed before retrying an operation. Defaults to 64000 (64 seconds).
* The amount of delay in milliseconds between retry attempts. Defaults to 1000 (1 second.) The delay increases exponentially with each retry up to a maximum specified by maxRetryDelayInMs.

* Header names whose values will be logged when logging is enabled. Defaults include a list of well-known safe headers. Any headers specified in this field will be added to that list. Any other values will be written to logs as "REDACTED".
* Query string names whose values will be logged when logging is enabled. By default no query string values are logged.
* The log function to use for writing pipeline logs. Defaults to core-http's built-in logger. Compatible with the `debug` library.
* Options for how redirect responses are handled.

* Whether to follow redirects to a different origin (scheme + host + port). When false (the default), cross-origin redirects are not followed and the redirect response is returned directly to the caller. Defaults to false.
* The maximum number of times the redirect URL will be tried before failing. Defaults to 20.

* A {@link RestError}, if the last retry attempt failed.
* Total number of retries so far.
* Properties that can modify the behavior of the retry policy.
* Indicates to throw this error instead of retrying.
* Indicates to retry against this URL.
* Controls whether to retry in a given number of milliseconds. If provided, a new retry will be attempted.
* If true, allows skipping the current strategy from running on the retry policy.
* Logger. If it's not provided, a default logger is used.
* Maximum number of retries. If not specified, it will limit to 3 retries.
* A retry strategy is intended to define whether to retry or not, and how to retry.
* Logger. If it's not provided, a default logger for all retry strategies is used.
* Name of the retry strategy. Used for logging.
